claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me:
- How many children you have?
- Their names and ages?

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. I have one child – Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

Now I need to ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Does Alex normally live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is an important factor in determining eligibility.

**Could you tell me more about Alex's living situation? Specifically:**
- Who does Alex live with?
- Do you contribute to Alex's upbringing in any way (such as financially or through regular care)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent. But yes, I do contribute financially – I help out with things like clothes, school expenses, and other costs for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. When a child doesn't live with you but lives with someone else (in this case, Alex's other parent), there are specific rules about Child Benefit eligibility.

Let me ask you this:

**Is the other parent (the one Alex lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is helpful information.

Let me ask you another important question:

**Even though Alex doesn't live with you full-time, do you provide for Alex in any of these ways:**
- Does Alex stay with you regularly (for example, on weekends or during holidays)?
- Do you provide a home for Alex at least part of the time?
- Or is your support purely financial (sending money/paying for things) without Alex actually staying with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My support is purely financial – I send money and pay for things Alex needs, but Alex doesn't stay with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the UK Child Benefit rules, I need to provide you with my assessment:

**Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment**

Unfortunately, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex under these circumstances.

Here's why:

**The key rules are:**
- Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child
- Generally, Child Benefit goes to the person the child **lives with**
- If a child doesn't live with you, you can only claim Child Benefit if you're contributing to the cost of providing for that child **and** the child lives with you at least some of the time, or you're providing a home for them

**In your situation:**
- Alex lives full-time with their other parent
- Alex doesn't stay with you at any time
- Your support is purely financial (money and paying for expenses)
- While your financial contribution is valuable, it doesn't meet the requirement that the child must live with you to claim the benefit

**What this means:**
The other parent (who Alex lives with) would be the person eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, even though they're not currently claiming it. You might want to encourage them to make a claim, as they could be missing out on financial support they're entitled to.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, so meets the age requirement. However, Alex does not live with the claimant - Alex lives full-time with their other parent. The claimant provides only financial support (money for clothes, school expenses, etc.) but Alex does not stay with the claimant at any time. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a child doesn't live with you, you can only claim if you contribute to the cost of providing for the child AND the child lives with you at least part of the time. Since Alex never stays with the claimant and the support is purely financial, the claimant does not meet the 'living with' requirement. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and it should be the person the child lives with - in this case, Alex's other parent.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives full-time with their other parent. The claimant provides financial support but Alex does not stay with them. Under Child Benefit rules, the benefit is paid to the person the child lives with. Since Alex does not live with the claimant even part-time, the claimant is not eligible. The other parent who Alex lives with would be the eligible person to claim.

The claimant provides only financial support (money for clothes, school expenses, etc.) but Alex does not stay with the claimant at any time. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a child doesn't live with you, you can only claim if you contribute to the cost of providing for the child AND the child lives with you at least part of the time. Since Alex never stays with the claimant and the support is purely financial, the claimant does not meet the 'living with' requirement. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and it should be the person the child lives with - in this case, Alex's other parent.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives full-time with their other parent. The claimant provides financial support but Alex does not stay with them. Under Child Benefit rules, the benefit is paid to the person the child lives with. Since Alex does not live with the claimant even part-time, the claimant is not eligible. The other parent who Alex lives with would be the eligible person to claim.
